ESV.1: And when we had parted from them and set sail, we came by a straight course to Cos, and the next day to Rhodes, and from there to Patara.
ESV.2: And having found a ship crossing to Phoenicia, we went aboard and set sail.
ESV.3: When we had come in sight of Cyprus, leaving it on the left we sailed to Syria and landed at Tyre, for there the ship was to unload its cargo.
ESV.4: And having sought out the disciples, we stayed there for seven days. And through the Spirit they were telling Paul not to go on to Jerusalem.
ESV.5: When our days there were ended, we departed and went on our journey, and they all, with wives and children, accompanied us until we were outside the city. And kneeling down on the beach, we prayed
ESV.6: and said farewell to one another. Then we went on board the ship, and they returned home.
ESV.7: When we had finished the voyage from Tyre, we arrived at Ptolemais, and we greeted the brothers  and stayed with them for one day.
ESV.8: On the next day we departed and came to Caesarea, and we entered the house of Philip the evangelist, who was one of the seven, and stayed with him.
ESV.9: He had four unmarried daughters, who prophesied.
ESV.10: While we were staying for many days, a prophet named Agabus came down from Judea.
ESV.11: And coming to us, he took Paul's belt and bound his own feet and hands and said, “Thus says the Holy Spirit, ‘This is how the Jews  at Jerusalem will bind the man who owns this belt and deliver him into the hands of the Gentiles.’”
ESV.12: When we heard this, we and the people there urged him not to go up to Jerusalem.
ESV.13: Then Paul answered, “What are you doing, weeping and breaking my heart? For I am ready not only to be imprisoned but even to die in Jerusalem for the name of the Lord Jesus.”
ESV.14: And since he would not be persuaded, we ceased and said, “Let the will of the Lord be done.”
ESV.15: After these days we got ready and went up to Jerusalem.
ESV.16: And some of the disciples from Caesarea went with us, bringing us to the house of Mnason of Cyprus, an early disciple, with whom we should lodge.
ESV.17: When we had come to Jerusalem, the brothers received us gladly.
ESV.18: On the following day Paul went in with us to James, and all the elders were present.
ESV.19: After greeting them, he related one by one the things that God had done among the Gentiles through his ministry.
ESV.20: And when they heard it, they glorified God. And they said to him, “You see, brother, how many thousands there are among the Jews of those who have believed. They are all zealous for the law,
ESV.21: and they have been told about you that you teach all the Jews who are among the Gentiles to forsake Moses, telling them not to circumcise their children or walk according to our customs.
ESV.22: What then is to be done? They will certainly hear that you have come.
ESV.23: Do therefore what we tell you. We have four men who are under a vow;
ESV.24: take these men and purify yourself along with them and pay their expenses, so that they may shave their heads. Thus all will know that there is nothing in what they have been told about you, but that you yourself also live in observance of the law.
ESV.25: But as for the Gentiles who have believed, we have sent a letter with our judgment that they should abstain from what has been sacrificed to idols, and from blood, and from what has been strangled,  and from sexual immorality.”
ESV.26: Then Paul took the men, and the next day he purified himself along with them and went into the temple, giving notice when the days of purification would be fulfilled and the offering presented for each one of them.
ESV.27: When the seven days were almost completed, the Jews from Asia, seeing him in the temple, stirred up the whole crowd and laid hands on him,
ESV.28: crying out, “Men of Israel, help! This is the man who is teaching everyone everywhere against the people and the law and this place. Moreover, he even brought Greeks into the temple and has defiled this holy place.”
ESV.29: For they had previously seen Trophimus the Ephesian with him in the city, and they supposed that Paul had brought him into the temple.
ESV.30: Then all the city was stirred up, and the people ran together. They seized Paul and dragged him out of the temple, and at once the gates were shut.
ESV.31: And as they were seeking to kill him, word came to the tribune of the cohort that all Jerusalem was in confusion.
ESV.32: He at once took soldiers and centurions and ran down to them. And when they saw the tribune and the soldiers, they stopped beating Paul.
ESV.33: Then the tribune came up and arrested him and ordered him to be bound with two chains. He inquired who he was and what he had done.
ESV.34: Some in the crowd were shouting one thing, some another. And as he could not learn the facts because of the uproar, he ordered him to be brought into the barracks.
ESV.35: And when he came to the steps, he was actually carried by the soldiers because of the violence of the crowd,
ESV.36: for the mob of the people followed, crying out, “Away with him!”
ESV.37: As Paul was about to be brought into the barracks, he said to the tribune, “May I say something to you?” And he said, “Do you know Greek?
ESV.38: Are you not the Egyptian, then, who recently stirred up a revolt and led the four thousand men of the Assassins out into the wilderness?”
ESV.39: Paul replied, “I am a Jew, from Tarsus in Cilicia, a citizen of no obscure city. I beg you, permit me to speak to the people.”
ESV.40: And when he had given him permission, Paul, standing on the steps, motioned with his hand to the people. And when there was a great hush, he addressed them in the Hebrew language,  saying:

NASB.1: When we had parted from them and had set sail, we ran a straight course to Cos and the next day to Rhodes and from there to Patara;

NASB.3: When we came in sight of Cyprus, leaving it on the left, we kept sailing to Syria and landed at Tyre; for there the ship was to unload its cargo.
NASB.4: After looking up the disciples, we stayed there seven days; and they kept telling Paul through the Spirit not to set foot in Jerusalem.
NASB.5: When our days there were ended, we left and started on our journey, while they all, with wives and children, escorted us until [we were] out of the city. After kneeling down on the beach and praying, we said farewell to one another.
NASB.6: Then we went on board the ship, and they returned home again.
NASB.7: When we had finished the voyage from Tyre, we arrived at Ptolemais, and after greeting the brethren, we stayed with them for a day.
NASB.8: On the next day we left and came to Caesarea, and entering the house of Philip the evangelist, who was one of the seven, we stayed with him.
NASB.9: Now this man had four virgin daughters who were prophetesses.
NASB.10: As we were staying there for some days, a prophet named Agabus came down from Judea.
NASB.11: And coming to us, he took Paul's belt and bound his own feet and hands, and said, 'This is what the Holy Spirit says: 'In this way the Jews at Jerusalem will bind the man who owns this belt and deliver him into the hands of the Gentiles.''
NASB.12: When we had heard this, we as well as the local residents [began] begging him not to go up to Jerusalem.
NASB.13: Then Paul answered, 'What are you doing, weeping and breaking my heart? For I am ready not only to be bound, but even to die at Jerusalem for the name of the Lord Jesus.'
NASB.14: And since he would not be persuaded, we fell silent, remarking, 'The will of the Lord be done!'
NASB.15: After these days we got ready and started on our way up to Jerusalem.
NASB.16: [Some] of the disciples from Caesarea also came with us, taking us to Mnason of Cyprus, a disciple of long standing with whom we were to lodge.
NASB.17: After we arrived in Jerusalem, the brethren received us gladly.
NASB.18: And the following day Paul went in with us to James, and all the elders were present.
NASB.19: After he had greeted them, he [began] to relate one by one the things which God had done among the Gentiles through his ministry.
NASB.20: And when they heard it they [began] glorifying God; and they said to him, 'You see, brother, how many thousands there are among the Jews of those who have believed, and they are all zealous for the Law;
NASB.21: and they have been told about you, that you are teaching all the Jews who are among the Gentiles to forsake Moses, telling them not to circumcise their children nor to walk according to the customs.
NASB.22: 'What, then, is [to be done]? They will certainly hear that you have come.
NASB.23: 'Therefore do this that we tell you. We have four men who are under a vow;
NASB.24: take them and purify yourself along with them, and pay their expenses so that they may shave their heads; and all will know that there is nothing to the things which they have been told about you, but that you yourself also walk orderly, keeping the Law.
NASB.25: 'But concerning the Gentiles who have believed, we wrote, having decided that they should abstain from meat sacrificed to idols and from blood and from what is strangled and from fornication.'
NASB.26: Then Paul took the men, and the next day, purifying himself along with them, went into the temple giving notice of the completion of the days of purification, until the sacrifice was offered for each one of them.
NASB.27: When the seven days were almost over, the Jews from Asia, upon seeing him in the temple, [began] to stir up all the crowd and laid hands on him,
NASB.28: crying out, 'Men of Israel, come to our aid! This is the man who preaches to all men everywhere against our people and the Law and this place; and besides he has even brought Greeks into the temple and has defiled this holy place.'
NASB.29: For they had previously seen Trophimus the Ephesian in the city with him, and they supposed that Paul had brought him into the temple.
NASB.30: Then all the city was provoked, and the people rushed together, and taking hold of Paul they dragged him out of the temple, and immediately the doors were shut.
NASB.31: While they were seeking to kill him, a report came up to the commander of the [Roman] cohort that all Jerusalem was in confusion.
NASB.32: At once he took along [some] soldiers and centurions and ran down to them; and when they saw the commander and the soldiers, they stopped beating Paul.
NASB.33: Then the commander came up and took hold of him, and ordered him to be bound with two chains; and he [began] asking who he was and what he had done.
NASB.34: But among the crowd some were shouting one thing [and] some another, and when he could not find out the facts because of the uproar, he ordered him to be brought into the barracks.
NASB.35: When he got to the stairs, he was carried by the soldiers because of the violence of the mob;
NASB.36: for the multitude of the people kept following them, shouting, 'Away with him!'
NASB.37: As Paul was about to be brought into the barracks, he said to the commander, 'May I say something to you?' And he said, 'Do you know Greek?

NASB.39: But Paul said, 'I am a Jew of Tarsus in Cilicia, a citizen of no insignificant city; and I beg you, allow me to speak to the people.'

NLT.1: After saying farewell to the Ephesian elders, we sailed straight to the island of Cos. The next day we reached Rhodes and then went to Patara.
NLT.2: There we boarded a ship sailing for Phoenicia.
NLT.3: We sighted the island of Cyprus, passed it on our left, and landed at the harbor of Tyre, in Syria, where the ship was to unload its cargo.
NLT.4: We went ashore, found the local believers, and stayed with them a week. These believers prophesied through the Holy Spirit that Paul should not go on to Jerusalem.
NLT.5: When we returned to the ship at the end of the week, the entire congregation, including women and children, left the city and came down to the shore with us. There we knelt, prayed,
NLT.6: and said our farewells. Then we went aboard, and they returned home.
NLT.7: The next stop after leaving Tyre was Ptolemais, where we greeted the brothers and sisters and stayed for one day.
NLT.8: The next day we went on to Caesarea and stayed at the home of Philip the Evangelist, one of the seven men who had been chosen to distribute food.
NLT.9: He had four unmarried daughters who had the gift of prophecy.
NLT.10: Several days later a man named Agabus, who also had the gift of prophecy, arrived from Judea.
NLT.11: He came over, took Paul’s belt, and bound his own feet and hands with it. Then he said, “The Holy Spirit declares, ‘So shall the owner of this belt be bound by the Jewish leaders in Jerusalem and turned over to the Gentiles.’”
NLT.12: When we heard this, we and the local believers all begged Paul not to go on to Jerusalem.
NLT.13: But he said, “Why all this weeping? You are breaking my heart! I am ready not only to be jailed at Jerusalem but even to die for the sake of the Lord Jesus.”
NLT.14: When it was clear that we couldn’t persuade him, we gave up and said, “The Lord’s will be done.”
NLT.15: After this we packed our things and left for Jerusalem.
NLT.16: Some believers from Caesarea accompanied us, and they took us to the home of Mnason, a man originally from Cyprus and one of the early believers.
NLT.17: When we arrived, the brothers and sisters in Jerusalem welcomed us warmly.
NLT.18: The next day Paul went with us to meet with James, and all the elders of the Jerusalem church were present.
NLT.19: After greeting them, Paul gave a detailed account of the things God had accomplished among the Gentiles through his ministry.
NLT.20: After hearing this, they praised God. And then they said, “You know, dear brother, how many thousands of Jews have also believed, and they all follow the law of Moses very seriously.
NLT.21: But the Jewish believers here in Jerusalem have been told that you are teaching all the Jews who live among the Gentiles to turn their backs on the laws of Moses. They’ve heard that you teach them not to circumcise their children or follow other Jewish customs.
NLT.22: What should we do? They will certainly hear that you have come.
NLT.23: “Here’s what we want you to do. We have four men here who have completed their vow.
NLT.24: Go with them to the Temple and join them in the purification ceremony, paying for them to have their heads ritually shaved. Then everyone will know that the rumors are all false and that you yourself observe the Jewish laws.
NLT.25: “As for the Gentile believers, they should do what we already told them in a letter: They should abstain from eating food offered to idols, from consuming blood or the meat of strangled animals, and from sexual immorality.”
NLT.26: So Paul went to the Temple the next day with the other men. They had already started the purification ritual, so he publicly announced the date when their vows would end and sacrifices would be offered for each of them.
NLT.27: The seven days were almost ended when some Jews from the province of Asia saw Paul in the Temple and roused a mob against him. They grabbed him,
NLT.28: yelling, “Men of Israel, help us! This is the man who preaches against our people everywhere and tells everybody to disobey the Jewish laws. He speaks against the Temple — and even defiles this holy place by bringing in Gentiles. ”
NLT.29: (For earlier that day they had seen him in the city with Trophimus, a Gentile from Ephesus, and they assumed Paul had taken him into the Temple.)
NLT.30: The whole city was rocked by these accusations, and a great riot followed. Paul was grabbed and dragged out of the Temple, and immediately the gates were closed behind him.
NLT.31: As they were trying to kill him, word reached the commander of the Roman regiment that all Jerusalem was in an uproar.
NLT.32: He immediately called out his soldiers and officers and ran down among the crowd. When the mob saw the commander and the troops coming, they stopped beating Paul.
NLT.33: Then the commander arrested him and ordered him bound with two chains. He asked the crowd who he was and what he had done.
NLT.34: Some shouted one thing and some another. Since he couldn’t find out the truth in all the uproar and confusion, he ordered that Paul be taken to the fortress.
NLT.35: As Paul reached the stairs, the mob grew so violent the soldiers had to lift him to their shoulders to protect him.
NLT.36: And the crowd followed behind, shouting, “Kill him, kill him!”
NLT.37: As Paul was about to be taken inside, he said to the commander, “May I have a word with you?” “Do you know Greek?” the commander asked, surprised.
NLT.38: “Aren’t you the Egyptian who led a rebellion some time ago and took 4,000 members of the Assassins out into the desert?”
NLT.39: “No,” Paul replied, “I am a Jew and a citizen of Tarsus in Cilicia, which is an important city. Please, let me talk to these people.”
NLT.40: The commander agreed, so Paul stood on the stairs and motioned to the people to be quiet. Soon a deep silence enveloped the crowd, and he addressed them in their own language, Aramaic.

AMP.1: When we had torn ourselves away from them and set sail, we ran a straight course and came to Cos, and on the next day to Rhodes, and from there to Patara;
AMP.2: and after finding a ship crossing over to Phoenicia, we went on board and set sail.
AMP.3: After we came in sight of Cyprus, leaving it on our left (port side), we sailed on to Syria and put in at Tyre; for there the ship was to unload her cargo.
AMP.4: After looking up the disciples [in Tyre], we stayed there [with them] seven days; and they kept telling Paul through the [Holy] Spirit not to set foot in Jerusalem.
AMP.5: When our days there came to an end, we left and proceeded on our journey, while all of the disciples, with their wives and children, escorted us on our way until we were outside the city. After kneeling down on the beach and praying, we told one another goodbye.
AMP.6: Then we boarded the ship, and they returned to their homes.
AMP.7: When we had completed the voyage from Tyre, we landed [twenty miles to the south] at Ptolemais, and after greeting the believers, we stayed with them for one day.
AMP.8: On the next day we left and came to Caesarea, and we went to the house of Philip the evangelist, who was one of the seven [deacons], and stayed with him.
AMP.9: He had four virgin daughters who had the gift of prophecy.
AMP.10: As we were staying there for some time, a prophet named Agabus came down from Judea.
AMP.11: And coming to [see] us, he took Paul's [wide] band (belt, sash) and bound his own feet and hands, and said, "This is what the Holy Spirit says: 'In this same way the Jews in Jerusalem will bind the man who owns this band, and they will hand him over to the Gentiles (pagans).'"
AMP.12: Now when we had heard this, both we and the local residents began pleading with Paul trying to persuade him not to go up to Jerusalem.
AMP.13: Then Paul replied, "What are you doing, weeping and breaking my heart [like this]? For I am ready not only to be bound and imprisoned, but even to die at Jerusalem for the name of the Lord Jesus."
AMP.14: And since he would not be dissuaded, we stopped pleading and fell silent, saying, "The Lord's will be done!"
AMP.15: After this we got ready and started on our way up to Jerusalem.
AMP.16: Some of the disciples from Caesarea also came with us, taking us to [the house of] Mnason, a man from Cyprus, a disciple of long standing with whom we were to lodge.
AMP.17: When we arrived in Jerusalem, the brothers and sisters welcomed us gladly.
AMP.18: On the next day Paul went with us to [see] James, and all the elders of the church were present.
AMP.19: After greeting them, Paul began to give a detailed account of the things that God had done among the Gentiles through his ministry.
AMP.20: And when they heard it, they began glorifying and praising God; and they said to him, "You see, brother, how many thousands of believers there are among the Jews, and they are all enthusiastic supporters of the Law [of Moses].
AMP.21: Now they have been told about you, that you are teaching all the Jews who are living among the Gentiles to turn away from [the Law of] Moses, advising them not to circumcise their children or to live according to the [Mosaic] customs.
AMP.22: What then should be done? They will certainly hear that you have arrived.
AMP.23: Therefore do just what we tell you. We have four men who have taken a vow;
AMP.24: take these men and purify yourself along with them, and pay their expenses [for the temple offerings] so that they may shave their heads. Then everyone will know that there is nothing to the things they have been told about you, but that you yourself also follow and keep the Law.
AMP.25: But with regard to the Gentiles who have believed [in Christ], we have sent them a letter with our decision that they should abstain from meat sacrificed to idols and from [consuming] blood and from [the meat of] what has been strangled and from sexual immorality."
AMP.26: Then Paul took the [four] men, and the next day he purified himself along with them [by submitting to the ritual]. He went into the temple to give notice of [the time] when the days of purification [ending each vow] would be fulfilled and the usual offering could be presented on behalf of each one.
AMP.27: When the seven days [required to complete the ritual] were almost over, [some] Jews from [the province of] Asia [Minor], caught sight of him in the temple, and began to stir up the crowd, and they seized him,
AMP.28: shouting, "Men of Israel, help us! This is the man who teaches all men everywhere against our people and the Law and this place. And besides, he has brought Greeks into the temple and has defiled this holy place."
AMP.29: For they had previously seen Trophimus the Ephesian in the city with Paul, and they assumed that he had brought the man into the temple [beyond the court of the Gentiles].
AMP.30: Then the whole city was provoked and confused, and the people rushed together. They seized Paul and dragged him out of the temple, and immediately the gates were closed.
AMP.31: Now while they were trying to kill him, word came to the commander of the [Roman] garrison that all Jerusalem was in a state of upheaval.
AMP.32: So he immediately took soldiers and centurions and ran down among them. When the people saw the commander and the soldiers, they stopped beating Paul.
AMP.33: Then the commander came up and arrested Paul, and ordered that he be bound with two chains. Then he asked who he was and what he had done.
AMP.34: But some in the crowd were shouting one thing and others something else; and since he could not determine the facts because of the uproar, he ordered that Paul be taken to the barracks [in the tower of Antonia].
AMP.35: When Paul got to the steps, he was carried by the soldiers because of the violence of the mob;
AMP.36: for the majority of the people kept following them, shouting, "Away with him! [Kill him!]"
AMP.37: Just as Paul was about to be taken into the barracks, he asked the commander, "May I say something to you?" And the man replied, "Do you know Greek?
AMP.38: Then you are not [as I assumed] the Egyptian who some time ago stirred up a rebellion and led those 4,000 men of the Assassins out into the wilderness?"
AMP.39: Paul said, "I am a Jew from Tarsus in Cilicia (Mersin Province, Turkey), a citizen of no insignificant city; and I beg you, allow me to speak to the people."
AMP.40: When the commander had given him permission, Paul, standing on the steps, gestured with his hand to the people; and when there was a great hush, he spoke to them in the Hebrew dialect (Jewish Aramaic), saying,

MSG.1: And so, with the tearful good-byes behind us, we were on our way. We made a straight run to Cos, the next day reached Rhodes, and then Patara.
MSG.2: There we found a ship going direct to Phoenicia, got on board, and set sail.
MSG.3: Cyprus came into view on our left, but was soon out of sight as we kept on course for Syria, and eventually docked in the port of Tyre. While the cargo was being unloaded,
MSG.4: we looked up the local disciples and stayed with them seven days. Their message to Paul, from insight given by the Spirit, was "Don't go to Jerusalem."
MSG.5: When our time was up, they escorted us out of the city to the docks. Everyone came along—men, women, children. They made a farewell party of the occasion! We all kneeled together on the beach and prayed.
MSG.6: Then, after another round of saying good-bye, we climbed on board the ship while they drifted back to their homes.
MSG.7: A short run from Tyre to Ptolemais completed the voyage. We greeted our Christian friends there and stayed with them a day.
MSG.8: In the morning we went on to Caesarea and stayed with Philip the Evangelist, one of "the Seven."
MSG.9: Philip had four virgin daughters who prophesied.
MSG.10: After several days of visiting, a prophet from Judea by the name of Agabus came down to see us.
MSG.11: He went right up to Paul, took Paul's belt, and, in a dramatic gesture, tied himself up, hands and feet. He said, "This is what the Holy Spirit says: The Jews in Jerusalem are going to tie up the man who owns this belt just like this and hand him over to godless unbelievers."
MSG.12: When we heard that, we and everyone there that day begged Paul not to be stubborn and persist in going to Jerusalem.
MSG.13: But Paul wouldn't budge: "Why all this hysteria? Why do you insist on making a scene and making it even harder for me? You're looking at this backwards. The issue in Jerusalem is not what they do to me, whether arrest or murder, but what the Master Jesus does through my obedience. Can't you see that?"
MSG.14: We saw that we weren't making even a dent in his resolve, and gave up. "It's in God's hands now," we said. "Master, you handle it."
MSG.15: It wasn't long before we had our luggage together and were on our way to Jerusalem.
MSG.16: Some of the disciples from Caesarea went with us and took us to the home of Mnason, who received us warmly as his guests. A native of Cyprus, he had been among the earliest disciples.
MSG.17: In Jerusalem, our friends, glad to see us, received us with open arms.
MSG.18: The first thing next morning, we took Paul to see James. All the church leaders were there.
MSG.19: After a time of greeting and small talk, Paul told the story, detail by detail, of what God had done among the Gentiles through his ministry.
MSG.20: They listened with delight and gave God the glory. They had a story to tell, too: "And just look at what's been happening here—thousands upon thousands of God-fearing Jews have become believers in Jesus! But there's also a problem because they are more zealous than ever in observing the laws of Moses.
MSG.21: They've been told that you advise believing Jews who live surrounded by Gentiles to go light on Moses, telling them that they don't need to circumcise their children or keep up the old traditions. This isn't sitting at all well with them.
MSG.22: "We're worried about what will happen when they discover you're in town. There's bound to be trouble. So here is what we want you to do:
MSG.23: There are four men from our company who have taken a vow involving ritual purification, but have no money to pay the expenses.
MSG.24: Join these men in their vows and pay their expenses. Then it will become obvious to everyone that there is nothing to the rumors going around about you and that you are in fact scrupulous in your reverence for the laws of Moses.
MSG.25: "In asking you to do this, we're not going back on our agreement regarding Gentiles who have become believers. We continue to hold fast to what we wrote in that letter, namely, to be careful not to get involved in activities connected with idols; to avoid serving food offensive to Jewish Christians; to guard the morality of sex and marriage."
MSG.26: So Paul did it—took the men, joined them in their vows, and paid their way. The next day he went to the Temple to make it official and stay there until the proper sacrifices had been offered and completed for each of them.
MSG.27: When the seven days of their purification were nearly up, some Jews from around Ephesus spotted him in the Temple. At once they turned the place upside-down. They grabbed Paul
MSG.28: and started yelling at the top of their lungs, "Help! You Israelites, help! This is the man who is going all over the world telling lies against us and our religion and this place. He's even brought Greeks in here and defiled this holy place."
MSG.29: (What had happened was that they had seen Paul and Trophimus, the Ephesian Greek, walking together in the city and had just assumed that he had also taken him to the Temple and shown him around.)
MSG.30: Soon the whole city was in an uproar, people running from everywhere to the Temple to get in on the action. They grabbed Paul, dragged him outside, and locked the Temple gates so he couldn't get back in and gain sanctuary.
MSG.31: As they were trying to kill him, word came to the captain of the guard, "A riot! The whole city's boiling over!"
MSG.32: He acted swiftly. His soldiers and centurions ran to the scene at once. As soon as the mob saw the captain and his soldiers, they quit beating Paul.
MSG.33: The captain came up and put Paul under arrest. He first ordered him handcuffed, and then asked who he was and what he had done.
MSG.34: All he got from the crowd were shouts, one yelling this, another that. It was impossible to tell one word from another in the mob hysteria, so the captain ordered Paul taken to the military barracks.
MSG.35: But when they got to the Temple steps, the mob became so violent that the soldiers had to carry Paul.
MSG.36: As they carried him away, the crowd followed, shouting, "Kill him! Kill him!"
MSG.37: When they got to the barracks and were about to go in, Paul said to the captain, "Can I say something to you?" He answered, "Oh, I didn't know you spoke Greek.
MSG.38: I thought you were the Egyptian who not long ago started a riot here, and then hid out in the desert with his four thousand thugs."
MSG.39: Paul said, "No, I'm a Jew, born in Tarsus. And I'm a citizen still of that influential city. I have a simple request: Let me speak to the crowd."
MSG.40: Standing on the barracks steps, Paul turned and held his arms up. A hush fell over the crowd as Paul began to speak. He spoke in Hebrew.

NET.27: When the seven days were almost over, the Jews from the province of Asia who had seen him in the temple area stirred up the whole crowd and seized him,
NET.28: shouting, “Men of Israel, help! This is the man who teaches everyone everywhere against our people, our law, and this sanctuary! Furthermore he has brought Greeks into the inner courts of the temple and made this holy place ritually unclean!”
NET.29: (For they had seen Trophimus the Ephesian in the city with him previously, and they assumed Paul had brought him into the inner temple courts.)
NET.30: The whole city was stirred up, and the people rushed together. They seized Paul and dragged him out of the temple courts, and immediately the doors were shut.
NET.31: While they were trying to kill him, a report was sent up to the commanding officer of the cohort that all Jerusalem was in confusion.
NET.32: He immediately took soldiers and centurions and ran down to the crowd. When they saw the commanding officer and the soldiers, they stopped beating Paul.
NET.33: Then the commanding officer came up and arrested him and ordered him to be tied up with two chains; he then asked who he was and what he had done.
NET.34: But some in the crowd shouted one thing, and others something else, and when the commanding officer was unable to find out the truth because of the disturbance, he ordered Paul to be brought into the barracks.
NET.35: When he came to the steps, Paul had to be carried by the soldiers because of the violence of the mob,
NET.36: for a crowd of people followed them, screaming, “Away with him!”
NET.37: As Paul was about to be brought into the barracks, he said to the commanding officer, “May I say something to you?” The officer replied, “Do you know Greek?
NET.38: Then you’re not that Egyptian who started a rebellion and led the four thousand men of the ‘Assassins’ into the wilderness some time ago?”
NET.39: Paul answered, “I am a Jew from Tarsus in Cilicia, a citizen of an important city. Please allow me to speak to the people.”
